course_description: This course is an introductory level examination of the basic principles of taxation law in Australia, with particular emphasis on the topics required by professional accounting bodies for accreditation purposes.

requirements.restrictions: Not to be studied in JD, LLM or LLM(Adv) programs Quota: Summer semester minimum of 30 enrolments
requirements.assumed_background: An understanding of Australia's legal system and how legislation and case law interact is assumed. It is also assumed that students will have a working knowledge of the accounting principles of income and expenditure. An understanding of how depreciation is calculated in accounting terms is also useful.

timetable:
Timetables are available on mySI-net.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims:
Teaching Mission Statement

The mission of the TC Beirne School of Law is to educate students in and about the law. We seek to assist you to think critically about the law and to investigate its structures, principles, policies, and values. This is achieved by engaging with you as fellow investigators in a range of critical and intellectual conversations about the law and its place in society. Our role is thus to guide you in your own intellectual journey in the law and to equip you with the necessary skills that will enable you to continue that journey long after you have left the School.

Course Aims

The overall aim of the course is to provide students with a comprehensive knowledge of the basic principles of income tax in Australia and how these principles are to be applied to businesses. It should be noted that it is not an objective of this course to study in detail all of the specific and technical provisions in the legislation which govern matters which affect the actual preparation of income tax returns.

learning_outcomes.description: Prove a knowledge of the regulatory framework of the taxation system in Australia.
Demonstrate an understanding of the income tax formula and the elements which are the basis of it and be able to apply those elements to the case of taxpayers operating in a business environment.

Interpret provisions of the relevant Income Tax Assessment Act and apply them by preparing a concise written argument sufficient to form the basis of sound advice to a taxpayer and/or calculating the financial implications of a particular transaction.
Critically evaluate case law and Rulings & Determinations issued by the Commissioner of Taxation.

Solve legal problems/issues relating to assessable income; deductions; capital gains tax; tax offsets; tax payable; Fringe Benefits Tax and the Goods & Services Tax; business structures such as partnerships, trusts and companies; taxation administration; and current issues in taxation.
Demonstrate the ability to research broad issues in the area of taxation law.
Discuss legal issues raised relating to taxation law in a confident and informed manner.

assessment_details.title: 7012 Assignment
assessment_details.task_description:
The assignment will contain 2 questions and is worth 40% of the assessment in this course.

The assignment will cover material from Topics 1 to 3 and involves research into various tax issues covered by these topics.

More details on the assignment, including detailed marking criteria will be provided in Week 4.

The assignment should be approximately 2000-3000 words in length.

The assignment must be submitted electronically via Turnitin on Blackboard.

The assignment will be released on Thursday 28 March.

The assignment will be due on Thursday 18 April before 4:00pm.
assessment_details.criteria_and_marking:
UQ Students: Please access the profile from
Learn.UQ

or
mySI-net

to access marking criteria held in this profile.

Submission:

The assignment must be submitted electronically via the 'Turnitin' link on the course Blackboard site.

assessment_details.title: Final Exam
assessment_details.task_description:
The final exam will cover material from all nine topics. The questions will be problem style questions.

Students may take any printed materials into the exam, including all of the prescribed textbooks (which may be annotated). Bi-lingual dictionaries are permitted, but no electronic devices will be allowed. Students will also need a University approved calculator.

Taxation texts other than those prescribed for this course will not be permitted into the final exam.

learning_resources.required_resources:
LAWS7012 Course Website (Blackboard). The LAWS7012 website contains most of the material you will require for this course. The website is in topic order and will contain where relevant, lecture notes, lecture slides, case studies and tutorial tasks plus other reference materials.

http://blackboard.elearning.uq.edu.au

Principles of Taxation Law (2013). Thomson. Sadiq, Coleman & Ors.

[This book can be purchased with a CD version of the Legislation and a copy of Australian Taxation Law Cases as Tax Kit 10 . It can also be purchased with just a CD copy of the Legislation as Tax Kit 11 .]

Australian Taxation Law Cases (2013). Thomson. Krever.

Fundamental Tax Legislation (2013). Thomson.

[This book can be purchased as a CD in either Tax Kit 10 or Tax Kit 11].
learning_resources.recommended_resources: Students may find resources such as the Australian Taxation Office (ATO) website and the ATO Legal Database helpful. There is a useful list of internet resources in Principles of Taxation Law at [2.480] resources.
learning_resources.university_learning_resources:
Access to required and recommended resources, plus past central exam papers, is available at the UQ Library website ( http://library.uq.edu.au/search/r?SEARCH=LAWS7012 ).

The University offers a range of resources and services to support student learning. Details are available on the myServices website ( https://student.my.uq.edu.au/ ).
learning_resources.school_learning_resources:
Law School Citation Style

Students preparing assignments, research papers, dissertations and theses are required to follow the Australian Guide to Legal Citation (3rd edition) .

The Law Library has produced a ‘How-to Guide’ to assist students to follow the AGLC3 citation style.

Law Library

The Law Library's Legal Research Guide site at http://www.library.uq.edu.au/subject-guides/legal-research-guide provides links to a wide range of Australian and International sites relevant to the study of law.

Students should refer to the UQ Library website generally for information relating to facilities, collections and services available - http://www.library.uq.edu.au .

Course Blackboard Website

Where the course utilises Blackboard , the Blackboard website will contain: course announcements; the course profile; the learning guide (where applicable); lecture slides; discussion boards; and intra-course email facilities. Students should visit the course Blackboard website regularly to check for course announcements and to read and contribute to any online activities.

The Course Coordinator will maintain and regularly update the course Blackboard website, which can be accessed via the following link – http://blackboard.elearn.uq.edu.au . It is your responsibility to ensure that you have access to the website . Access to Blackboard is automatically generated for those students enrolled through my SI-net approximately 1 week prior to the commencement of classes. If you do not have access in the first week of classes please contact the Law School Webmaster by email – webmaster@law.uq.edu.au .

AskIT , available through the University of Queensland Library, offers computing help and training free to UQ students. Contact the AskIT desk at Level 2 of the Duhig Building at St Lucia, or Building 8 at Ipswich - http://askit.uq.edu.au .
learning_resources.other_learning_resources:
The course website will contain all necessary resources and information not included in the prescribed texts.

Previous 2. Aims

Next 4. Activities

learning_activities.topic: Mon - Fri Topic 1 | Taxation System in Australia : An introduction to Australia's taxation system. We also examine the basic structure of the tax legislation. We conclude with an examination of the impact of residency on taxation within Australia. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 4 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 1); PoTL [2.480]; PoTL; ATLC; Legislation

Mon - Fri Topic 2 | Income (1) : The first lecture will consider the nature of income and its relevance to the taxation equation. In particular the meaning of assessable income and its distinction from capital receipts. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 2); PoTL; ATLC; Legislation

Mon - Fri Tutorial 1 | Topic 1 : Participation in a tutorial based session. Discuss solutions to tutorial problems posted on course website. Learning Objectives: 5, 6, 7 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 1); PoTL

Mon - Fri Topic 2 | Income (2) : The second lecture will examine business income and how certain business transactions may generate assessable income. We will review certain types of income specifically included in assessable income in accordance with the Income Tax Assessment Act. We also review the concept of exempt income. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 2); PoTL; ATLC; Legislation

Mon - Fri Tutorial 2 | Topic 2 : Participation in a tutorial based session. Discuss solutions to tutorial problems posted on course website. Learning Objectives: 5, 6, 7 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 2); PoTL

Mon - Fri Topic 3 | Capital Gains Tax (1) : This first lecture will introduce the concepts of CGT. An examination of exemptions and concessions available to taxpayers, including business entities is also included. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 3); PoTL; Legislation

Mon - Fri Tutorial 3 | Topic 2 : Participation in a tutorial based session. Discuss solutions to tutorial problems posted on course website. Learning Objectives: 5, 6, 7 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 2); PoTL

Mon - Fri Topic 3 | Capital Gains Tax (2) : This second lecture examines how to calculate capital gains and losses. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 3); PoTL; Legislation
Mon - Fri No Tutorials Scheduled : There are no tutorials scheduled for Week 5. Learning Objectives: 5, 6, 7
Mon - Sun Mid-Semester Break : No classes scheduled.

Mon - Fri Topic 4 | Deductions (1) : In the first lecture we will consider how certain expenditures can be used to reduce assessable income. The importance of deductions in the taxation equation is examined. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 4); PoTL; ATLC; Legislation

Mon - Fri Tutorial 4 | Topic 3 : Participation in a tutorial based session. Discuss solutions to tutorial problems posted on course website. Learning Objectives: 5, 6, 7 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 3); PoTL

Mon - Fri Topic 4 | Deductions (2) : The final lecture in the Deductions series examines specific deductions including the concept of depreciation (capital allowances) from a taxation perspective and how deductions relating to motor vehicle expenses are calculated in accordance with the provisions of the Income Tax Assessment Act. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 4); PoTL; Legislation

Mon - Fri Tutorial 5 | Topic 3 : Participation in a tutorial based session. Discuss solutions to tutorial problems posted on course website. Learning Objectives: 5, 6, 7 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 3); PoTL
Thu 16:00 Assignment Due (Assignment) : The LAWS7012 assignment is due at 4:00pm on Thursday 18 April. Learning Objectives: 3, 4, 6 Readings/Ref: Course Website; PoTL [2.480]

Mon - Fri Topic 5 | Income Tax Accounting : This topic will examine when income is derived (or earned) thereby becoming assessable income. Similarily, we examine when deductions are incurred (or expensed). We examine the concept of Cash v Accrual accounting from a taxation perspective. Also included is the nature and valuation of trading stock for income tax purposes. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 5); PoTL; ATLC; Legislation
Mon - Fri No Tutorials Scheduled : There are no tutorials scheduled for Week 8. Learning Objectives: 5, 6, 7

Mon - Fri Topic 6 | The Tax Assessment : This topic brings together all elements of the Tax Formula. We examine how to calculate tax payable on taxable income. We review the issuing of a tax assessment and some of the administrative practices of the ATO. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 5 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 6); PoTL; Legislation

Mon - Fri Tutorial 6 | Topic 4 : Participation in a tutorial based session. Discuss solutions to tutorial problems posted on course website. Learning Objectives: 5, 6, 7 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 4); PoTL

Mon - Fri Topic 7 | Goods & Services Tax : This topic will consider Australia's indirect taxation regime, in particular the Goods & Services Tax (GST). Learning Objectives: 1, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 7); PoTL; Legislation

Mon - Fri Tutorial 7 | Topic 5 : Participation in a tutorial based session. Discuss solutions to tutorial problems posted on course website. Learning Objectives: 5, 6, 7 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 5); PoTL

Mon - Fri Topic 8 | Fringe Benefits Tax : This topic examines the taxation issues associated with employing staff. In particular we examine Fringe Benefit Tax issues. Learning Objectives: 1, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 8); PoTL; ATLC; Legislation

Mon - Fri Tutorial 8 | Topic 6 : Participation in a tutorial based session. Discuss solutions to tutorial problems posted on course website. Learning Objectives: 5, 6, 7 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 6); PoTL

Mon - Fri Topic 9 | Business Structures (1) : This topic will consider the specific sections of the Income Tax Assessment Act which apply to various business structures. In the first lecture we examine the taxation issues when starting a business. We also examine the taxation consequences of using a partnership as a business structure. Learning Objectives: 1, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 9); PoTL; ATLC; Legislation

Mon - Fri Tutorial 9 | Topics 7 & 8 : Participation in a tutorial based session. Discuss solutions to tutorial problems posted on course website. Learning Objectives: 5, 6, 7 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topics 7 & 8); PoTL

Mon - Fri Topic 9 | Business Structures (2) : In the second lecture we examine the taxation consequences of using a trust or a company as a business structure. Learning Objectives: 1, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 9); PoTL; ATLC; Legislation

Mon - Fri Tutorial 10 | Topic 9 : Participation in a tutorial based session. Discuss solutions to tutorial problems posted on course website. Learning Objectives: 5, 6, 7 Readings/Ref: Course Website (Topic 9); PoTL

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the University’s assessment-related policies can be found on myAdvisor ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2910 ).

Academic Integrity

It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Plagiarism

The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Student Integrity and Misconduct policy ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.60.04-student-integrity-and-misconduct ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Applications for Extensions

Medical grounds: Applications for extension on medical grounds shall be made by lodging the Application for Extension of Progressive Assessment form and supporting documentation at the location outlined in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile for the course concerned.

Exceptional circumstances: Applications for extension on the grounds of exceptional circumstances shall be made to the relevant Course Coordinator/Program Director by lodging the Application for Extension of Progressive Assessment form and a personal statement outlining the grounds for the application at the location and by the due date outlined in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile for the course in which this application is made. If the exceptional circumstances are such that the student cannot reasonably be expected to have complied with these conditions, a case should be made as to why these conditions could not be met.

Outcome of application: Students will be advised of the outcome of their application via their student email.

Feedback on Assessment Feedback is essential to effective learning and students can expect to receive appropriate and timely feedback on all assessment. For a detailed explanation of the feedback you are entitled to, you should consult the policy on Student Access to Feedback on Assessment. ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.02-assessment )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the myAdvisor web site. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2953 )

Feedback in this Course

A Student Feedback document will be available for both the 7012 Assignment and the Final Exam.

School of Law
Assessment Guidelines

Communication with Examiners

Students who have completed an item of assessment should not contact the examiners about that item of assessment prior to the release of the grades or marks for that item of assessment. For queries of an administrative nature, please contact Law School Reception.

After the release of the final grades for the course, feedback may be sought on examination performance in accordance with the School’s policy ( http://www.law.uq.edu.au/obtaining-feedback-on-final-examinations).

Special Examinations

Centrally Controlled Special Examinations

Students should refer to myAdvisor for the policy relating to centrally controlled special examinations - http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2910

School Special Examinations

Where a student does not attend a mid-semester examination, the student will in the normal course of events receive zero marks for the examination.

Where, due to a medical condition occurring on the day of the examination, a student is unable to attend a mid-semester examination, or the performance of a student in the examination has been impaired, the student may, on the presentation of a certificate covering the date of the examination from a registered medical practitioner and a written explanation of how the medical condition prevented attendance/impaired performance at the examination, be permitted to sit a special examination in lieu.

For full details, students should refer to the School policy on Special Mid-Semester Examinations .

Applications for a special mid-semester examination shall be made by lodging the Application for Special Mid-Semester Examination form (and supporting documentation) with the Law School Office within 5 days following the date of the mid-semester examination.

Special mid-semester examinations will be held in the period nominated by the School for the holding of mid-semester special examinations and may take the format of an oral examination.

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses

Students on a placement course – also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice – should refer to the University policy, Placement Courses ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.04-placement-courses ) for detailed information.

Working with Children

Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/1.60.07-working-children ) to find out how to apply for a ‘blue card’.

Students with a Disability

Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Advisor at Student Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.08-alternative-academic-arrangements-students-disability ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.09-arrangements-reasonable-adjustments-examinations-students-disability#Procedures ).

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety

Undergraduate and Postgraduate Students should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/2.30.14-occupational-health-and-safety-laboratory ).

Other
School of Law
Guidelines

Responsibilities of Students

Students are expected to take responsibility for their own learning and academic development. This includes:

devoting sufficient time and effort to your studies to make and maintain adequate progress,

preparing for lectures and tutorials,

reading prescribed materials,

undertaking such independent study as necessary to reach an understanding of the materials,

completion and submission of assessment items on time,

compliance with School, Faculty, and University rules.

Student Workload

For each 2 unit course students are expected to undertake at least 7 – 8 hours per week of independent study in addition to the time spent in lectures and tutorials.

Student and Staff Communication

If you email a staff member directly, you must include in your email your name, your student number and the name of the course you are enquiring about. Courtesy is always appreciated.

If you telephone a staff member directly and leave a message on their voicemail, you must provide contact details other than a mobile telephone number except where this is absolutely unavoidable.

While the use of telephone and email is acceptable for brief communications with a staff member, you should not expect detailed responses to questions about the law. These questions should be raised on the discussion board, in tutorial sessions or in staff consultation sessions.

Law School Website

Students should refer to the Law School website for more information including enrolment information and important dates; student announcements; news and events; information on Law School prizes and scholarships etc – http://www.law.uq.edu.au .

Previous 5. Assessment

Next Summary
course_introduction:
Income tax law is a complex and continually changing area of law. This course is designed to provide a broad understanding of the major topics which affect business taxpayers generally. The emphasis throughout will be on practical concepts with appropriate weight being given to the research of both legal questions and recent developments in the law.

The overall aim of the course is to provide students with a comprehensive knowledge of the basic principles of taxation in Australia and how these principles are to be applied to businesses. It should be noted that it is not an objective of this course to study in detail all of the specific and technical provisions in the legislation which govern matters which affect the actual preparation of income tax returns.

The Tax Institute Prizes in Law (Prize 3): The student with the highest overall marks in their first attempt at LAWS7012 will be recommended for this prize.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

The course structure has changed to a weekly two (2) hour lecture plus ten (10) one hour tutorials throughout the semester.

other_learning_activities_information:
The Function of Lectures

Lectures are a forum where, within an analytical framework offered by the lecturer, students and lecturer can:

Explore concepts of law relevant to the topic,

Explore how the particular concepts of law work, and relate to other concepts learned,

Identify the central conceptual, intellectual, and doctrinal questions raised by the material under consideration.

In a negative sense, lectures are not for:

Delivering an exclusive and exhaustive account of the material that the student needs to learn,

Reading material that students can read for themselves.

The Function of the Tutorial Program

Tutorials offer students an interactive forum within which to explore in more detail and in greater depth the issues introduced in lectures and to engage with the tutorial staff and other students in exploring those issues. Tutorials also offer students an opportunity to develop their skills in legal reasoning and argument.

As an interactive learning forum, the tutorial is dependent upon all students having prepared the material to be considered and being prepared to engage in debate.

Previous 3. Resources

Next 5. Assessment
course_grading:
Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Fail: Fails to demonstrate most or all of the basic requirements of the course:

1% - 19%

Grade 2, Fail: Demonstrates clear deficiencies in understanding and applying fundamental concepts; communicates information or ideas in ways that are frequently incomplete or confusing and give little attention to the conventions of the discipline:

20% - 47%

Grade 3, Fail: Demonstrates superficial or partial or faulty understanding of the fundamental concepts of the field of study and limited ability to apply these concepts; presents undeveloped or inappropriate or unsupported arguments; communicates information or ideas with lack of clarity and inconsistent adherence to the conventions of the discipline:

48% - 49%

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrates adequate understanding and application of the fundamental concepts of the field of study; develops routine arguments or decisions and provides acceptable justification; communicates information and ideas adequately in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

50% - 64%

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrates substantial understanding of fundamental concepts of the field of study and ability to apply these concepts in a variety of contexts; develops or adapts convincing arguments and provides coherent justification; communicates information and ideas clearly and fluently in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

65% - 74%

Grade 6, Distinction: As for 5, with frequent evidence of originality in defining and analysing issues or problems and in creating solutions; uses a level, style and means of communication appropriate to the discipline and the audience:

75% - 84%

Grade 7, High Distinction: As for 6, with consistent evidence of substantial originality and insight in identifying, generating and communicating competing arguments, perspectives or problem solving approaches; critically evaluates problems, their solutions and implications:

85% +
late_submission:
Where a non-examination item of assessment (including essays, assignments, take-home tests, etc) is not submitted by the due date and time, a penalty will be levied at the rate of 10% of the marks available for the assessment item per day (or part thereof) that the assessment is overdue.

Assessment submitted more than 5 days after the due date will receive zero marks. For the purposes of clarification, if the due date is the 10th, any work submitted on the 16th or later will receive zero marks. If the 6th day following the due date is a Saturday or a Sunday, assessment will be accepted until 9am on the following Monday.

No extension granted may exceed 14 days.

For full details, students should refer to the Law School's policy on Late Submission of Assessment . Except in exceptional circumstances, extensions for assessment will only be considered in respect of a certified medical condition . Applications for extension shall be made by lodging the Application for Extension form (and supporting documentation) at the Law School Office by the due date for assessment unless the exceptional circumstance or medical condition is such that the student cannot reasonably be expected to have applied prior to the due date.

Students who are granted an extension beyond the due date for assessment items must submit their assessment to the Law School Office, NOT the BEL Faculty Collaborative Learning Centre.
other_assessment_information: All assignments are now to be electronically submitted via Blackboard. The instructions for submission are in the Online Submission Folder in your course Blackboard site. The online submission is in addition to any other submission requirements that appear in this ECP.
